core.when: When estimating daily returns on high-volume trading days
core.action: Apply slippage penalty as negative deduction to returns proportional to traded value

consequence.description: Omitting slippage deductions on high-volume days produces inflated return estimates that do not reflect actual execution costs, leading to over-optimistic strategy assessments.

core.when: When clearing the market and computing price impact
core.action: save oldPrices BEFORE updating prices so that settlement uses the correct pre-impact price for calculating proceeds

consequence.description: Settlement will use incorrect price reference, causing wrong cash proceeds calculations and breaking conservation of value across the edge

core.when: When implementing time-series feature extraction
core.action: Validate that feature calculations use only data available at prediction time; implement explicit feature horizon constraints with timestamp validation to prevent lookahead bias

consequence.description: Features with lookahead bias cause strategies to trade on information not yet available, producing unrealistic backtest results; live trading will underperform by the magnitude of the bias

core.when: When interpreting haircut Sharpe ratio results
core.action: Claim adjusted Sharpe ratio guarantees live trading performance

consequence.description: Presenting backtest-adjusted Sharpe ratios as guaranteed live performance misleads stakeholders and violates financial regulation principles

core.when: When implementing any currency or monetary value handling in the trading system
core.action: Use Python Decimal type with explicit precision context for each currency calculations — convert float price data to Decimal before monetary arithmetic, and format output as string to avoid float representation issues

consequence.description: Without explicit Decimal handling, currency calculations suffer from float rounding errors that cause account balance discrepancies, incorrect P&L reporting, and potential margin calculation failures in live trading

core.when: When implementing monetary calculations in the backtesting engine
core.action: Use Python Decimal type for each currency calculations — import from decimal import Decimal; initialize with string or Decimal('X.XX') to avoid float conversion; apply Decimal throughout trade cost, P&L, and portfolio value calculations

consequence.description: Without Decimal, float-based currency calculations cause silent rounding errors that accumulate in high-frequency or long-running backtests, leading to incorrect strategy performance metrics

core.when: When converting daily or periodic returns to annualized equivalents for performance comparison
core.action: Use 240 trading days annualization factor for equity markets; verify market-specific holiday calendars for non-standard markets and adjust accordingly

consequence.description: Annualizing with 240 days may under/overestimate for markets with different holiday schedules or trading frequencies, causing unfair strategy comparison across markets

core.when: When estimating QDII fund net asset value for next-day trading
core.action: Use US market close prices and FX rates for T-1 NAV estimation — do not attempt same-day QDII estimation as US market data is unavailable during China trading hours

consequence.description: Attempting same-day QDII NAV estimation fails due to US market data unavailability, causing stale price execution that systematically misprices QDII fund trades by 0.5-2% on volatile days

core.when: When presenting feature importance results to stakeholders
core.action: claim that backtested importance rankings equal expected live trading performance

consequence.description: Feature importance rankings derived from historical backtests do not account for market regime changes, transaction costs, and execution slippage that materially affect live trading results

core.when: When implementing feature extraction logic for quantitative signals
core.action: Assume the framework handles time boundary conditions for feature extraction — the framework does not implement lookahead bias protection or explicit temporal boundary validation

consequence.description: Without explicit time boundary handling, feature extraction inadvertently uses future data (lookahead bias), causing backtest results that cannot be replicated in live trading

core.when: When validating Sharpe ratio results
core.action: Present infinite Sharpe ratio (from zero-variance returns) as valid performance metrics

consequence.description: Infinite Sharpe ratio from flat-line returns misrepresents strategy quality, creating false confidence in strategies with no volatility and potentially no returns

core.when: When using bet sizing outputs in backtesting
core.action: claim backtest bet sizes equal expected live trading performance

consequence.description: Backtest bet sizing does not account for slippage, market impact, or execution delays that occur in live trading, causing expected returns to be significantly overestimated

core.when: When accessing the framework as a user
core.action: Claim that this framework supports real-time trading execution — bt is a backtesting-only framework with no live trading connectivity or execution capabilities

consequence.description: Users allocate real capital expecting live execution, but the system only simulates historical trading, causing complete failure to execute any live trades

core.when: When backtest results are compared to live trading performance
core.action: Claim that backtest returns accurately predict live trading returns without accounting for slippage, fees, and execution latency

consequence.description: Users receive misleading performance expectations when backtest results are presented as expected live trading returns, leading to insufficient risk management

core.when: When implementing mortality rate calculations
core.action: Store mortality rates in per-mille convention (qx*1000) and maintain this format throughout calculations — do not normalize to decimal representation (0-1 scale)

consequence.description: Converting qx from per-mille to decimal representation introduces systematic factor-of-1000 errors in all premium and reserve calculations, causing severe mispricing in insurance products

core.when: When acquiring database connections from the pool
core.action: Return connections to the pool after use with putconn()

consequence.description: Connection leak exhausts the pool, causing subsequent operations to block indefinitely waiting for available connections

core.when: When using price indicators for trading decisions
core.action: Claim that backtest returns equal expected live trading returns

consequence.description: Presenting backtest results as expected live trading returns misleads users about actual strategy performance, ignoring slippage, latency, and market impact realities

core.when: When producing cleaned data output
core.action: Claim that backtest results equal expected live trading performance

consequence.description: Presenting backtest returns as expected live returns ignores transaction costs, slippage, execution delays, and liquidity constraints that only manifest in live trading

core.when: When constructing transaction graphs from multiple data sources with timestamps
core.action: Annotate each timestamps with explicit timezone identifiers and normalize to UTC before graph construction — convert local timestamps using source timezone metadata and store as UTC-aware datetime objects

consequence.description: Missing UTC normalization causes cross-timezone transaction graphs to have incorrect temporal ordering; alerts relying on chronological sequences may trigger at wrong times or miss detection windows entirely

core.when: When displaying backtested or sandbox rebalancing results
core.action: present results as guaranteed live trading outcomes

consequence.description: Sandbox API responses are simulated and may not reflect actual market conditions, order fills, or slippage; presenting them as live results misleads users about expected performance

core.when: When presenting backtesting results
core.action: claim backtest returns equal expected live trading returns

consequence.description: Backtest results exclude slippage from order delays, market impact from large orders, and liquidity constraints that cause live returns to deviate significantly from simulated results

core.when: When processing any monetary values (prices, scores, thresholds) in the trading system
core.action: Use Python float type for financial quantities — float's binary floating-point representation causes precision errors in decimal financial calculations

consequence.description: Using float for financial values (prices, scores, thresholds) causes accumulated rounding errors that can result in incorrect trade sizing, miscalculated P&L, or threshold breaches that violate strategy parameters

core.when: When presenting simulation results as execution proof
core.action: Present simulated wallet or backtest fills as proof of real execution capability

consequence.description: Users would be misled into believing strategies perform identically in live trading without understanding fill model assumptions and market differences

core.when: When presenting transaction analysis results, the system MUST NOT claim that backtest turnover rates predict live trading costs
core.action: Acknowledge in documentation that backtest slippage estimates are approximations based on historical volume and may not reflect actual execution quality

consequence.description: Overstating the accuracy of backtest slippage estimates leads users to expect live trading will match simulated costs, causing disappointment and potential financial losses

core.when: When calculating date ranges or time-based features for financial time series analysis
core.action: Assume the framework handles trading calendar distinctions — the framework does not distinguish between trading days and natural calendar days; using natural calendar dates with trading day logic causes off-by-one errors in feature calculation

consequence.description: Without trading calendar awareness, date-based features computed on natural calendar days produce incorrect results when market holidays or weekends create gaps, causing systematic feature calculation errors in backtesting and live trading

core.when: When comparing backtested swap or swaption prices to live trading results
core.action: claim that backtest prices represent expected live execution prices

consequence.description: Backtest prices are model-based estimates assuming execution at mid-market rates without transaction costs, bid-ask spreads, or market impact; live execution will differ materially due to slippage and timing

core.when: When encountering rows that fail numeric conversion
core.action: Delete or drop rows containing NaN values immediately after conversion, as this discards potentially valid data and creates survivorship bias in financial time series

consequence.description: Dropping NaN rows removes trading days with unusual conditions, creating incomplete historical records that over-represent normal market conditions and under-represent market stress periods

core.when: When parsing JSON responses from exchange APIs containing financial fields
core.action: Use Decimal type for each financial fields (price, quantity, volume) during JSON deserialization; do not rely on default float parsing which causes precision loss that propagates through calculations

consequence.description: Float parsing loses precision in financial calculations, causing rounding errors that accumulate in P&L calculations and position sizing, resulting in strategies producing incorrect trading signals

core.when: When generating reports without position or transaction data, the system MUST NOT claim live trading capability
core.action: clearly indicate in output that results are from backtest simulation, not live trading

consequence.description: Presenting backtest results as live trading performance violates financial analysis ethics and can mislead investors about actual expected returns

core.when: When aligning asset price data with trading signals in backtesting
core.action: Use left join with forward-fill (fill direction='left') for asset-signal alignment — left join preserves asset observations while forward-fill ensures alignment without introducing look-ahead bias; inner join drops assets without signals and right join drops signal observations

consequence.description: Using inner join drops assets without signals causing survivorship bias; using right join drops signal observations; incorrect join type introduces data leakage or survivorship bias in backtest results

core.when: When calculating risk-adjusted performance metrics for strategy evaluation
core.action: Use Sharpe ratio as the primary risk-adjusted return metric — do not substitute with Sortino ratio or information ratio as the default comparator

consequence.description: Using Sortino ratio instead of Sharpe ratio focuses only on downside volatility, missing the risk-adjusted comparison standard used across the industry and causing inconsistent strategy ranking

core.when: When implementing feature calculation logic in the market data gateway
core.action: Convert zero-sum rows to NaN during factor calculations — suspended trading days must not produce factor signals from zero-volume data

consequence.description: Without NaN conversion, zero-sum rows from suspended trading days create misleading factor signals that propagate through the entire backtest, causing strategies to behave unrealistically around market suspensions

core.when: When computing correlation matrix from covariance for HRP input
core.action: round correlation matrix to 6 decimal places to avoid floating-point artifacts

consequence.description: Unrounded correlation values may contain floating-point precision errors that corrupt distance matrix computation

core.when: When presenting factor regression results
core.action: claim that backtest regression coefficients predict future stock performance or guarantee investment returns

consequence.description: Past factor loadings do not guarantee future results; regression captures historical relationships that may change due to market regime shifts, structural breaks, or factor decay

core.when: When configuring fee parameters for backtesting accuracy
core.action: Verify that 'worst-case' fee selection matches your actual exchange fee tier and rebate structure; document the specific fee values used and compare against real trading confirmations to verify backtest realism

consequence.description: Worst-case fee assumption may not match actual trading costs; high-volume traders with fee rebates face different economics than the worst-case assumption, leading to strategies that appear profitable in backtest but underperform live

core.when: When presenting synthetic data generation results
core.action: Present synthetic backtest results as evidence of real trading or actuarial performance

consequence.description: Synthetic data reflects idealized assumptions and cannot substitute for empirical evidence; presenting it as proof violates financial analysis standards

core.when: When calculating date-based offsets or duration for trading strategies
core.action: Assume natural calendar calculations apply to trading strategies — natural calendar ignores non-trading days (weekends, holidays) and produces incorrect date offsets for trading operations

consequence.description: Using natural calendar (e.g., 30 days) for trading operations causes position calculations to span wrong number of trading days, corrupting time-series alignment and causing signals to fire on non-trading days

core.when: When querying skill-specific backtest summaries
core.action: Return None when no skill-tagged backtest summaries exist in storage

consequence.description: get_skill_summary intentionally returns None because the storage layer only persists overall and per-stock rollups. Returning a fabricated skill-specific summary would fabricate performance metrics and mislead auto-weighting systems into believing skill-specific data exists when it does not.

core.when: When selecting the best model in ensemble strategy
core.action: Use Sharpe ratio calculated on validation window to compare models

consequence.description: Without Sharpe ratio validation, the ensemble may select an overfitted model that performed well on training data but lacks generalization capability, leading to poor live trading performance

core.when: When calculating Debt-to-Equity ratios for leverage analysis
core.action: Calculate Debt-to-Equity using total liabilities divided by total equity; be aware that operating leases and off-balance sheet items are not included and require manual adjustment for comprehensive leverage measurement

consequence.description: Using inconsistent debt definitions changes leverage interpretation, causing incorrect risk assessments and potentially leading to over-leveraged positions or missed investment opportunities based on misleading leverage ratios

core.when: When parsing financial monetary values in SEC filings
core.action: Use Decimal type for monetary amounts to avoid floating-point precision errors

consequence.description: Floating-point arithmetic produces rounding errors in financial calculations, leading to incorrect currency values in extracted data that may cause downstream analysis errors

core.when: When using finmarketpy backtest results for regulatory or compliance reporting
core.action: represent simulated backtest P&L as proof of actual trading performance without independent verification

consequence.description: Presenting backtest-only results as verified trading records violates compliance requirements and can constitute misleading marketing, exposing the practitioner to regulatory action and reputational damage

core.when: When using Zipline's simulation framework
core.action: Assume that performance metrics (Sharpe ratio, returns) computed during backtest are guarantees of future trading success

consequence.description: Past performance metrics do not account for changing market conditions, regime shifts, or mean reversion of strategy effectiveness, leading to overconfidence in strategy viability

core.when: When implementing order execution logic in backtesting
core.action: Execute sell orders before buy orders when both are pending — verify no reliance on leverage or intraday credit

consequence.description: Buying before selling creates hidden leverage when capital is insufficient; in live trading, buy orders may be rejected due to insufficient funds, causing backtest-live inconsistency

core.when: When training models with early stopping
core.action: Use separate TRAIN and VALID data segments to prevent look-ahead bias in early stopping

consequence.description: Training on validation data inflates performance metrics, causing early stopping to trigger on data it was trained on, producing overfitted models with inflated backtest results

core.when: When running backtested order execution strategies
core.action: claim that simulated implementation shortfall equals expected live trading costs

consequence.description: Backtest simulations cannot account for real-world factors like slippage, latency, market impact feedback loops, and liquidity constraints, causing live results to significantly diverge from backtested performance

core.when: When running pipeline backtests
core.action: Claim that backtest returns equal expected live trading returns

consequence.description: Presenting backtest results as proof of live trading performance violates financial best practices; execution prices, slippage, and market impact differ between backtesting and live trading

core.when: When evaluating rebalancing decisions
core.action: Claim that backtested rebalancing returns predict live trading performance

consequence.description: Presenting simulated rebalancing results as expected live returns violates regulatory guidance and misleads clients about actual investment outcomes

core.when: When converting adjustment data dates
core.action: set utc=False to maintain naive datetime without timezone information

consequence.description: Timezone-aware datetime causes misalignment between adjustment dates and market data timestamps during join operations

core.when: When offering this software to end clients or making public claims about the system
core.action: Present simulated or sandbox test results as proof of live trading capability — the system is offered as-is without guarantees

consequence.description: Clients make capital allocation decisions based on unverified backtest or test environment results, leading to unexpected live trading outcomes
